draft.introduction: Psalm 40 moves from thanksgiving for past deliverance to urgent petition for present help. Its declaration that God desires obedience rather than sacrifice was cited in Hebrews 10 as prophesying Christ’s self-offering.

translation.text: I waited patiently for the Lord; he inclined to me and heard my cry. He drew me up from the pit of destruction, out of the miry bog, and set my feet upon a rock. Sacrifice and offering you have not desired, but you have given me an open ear. Burnt offering and sin offering you have not required. Then I said, ‘Behold, I have come; in the scroll of the book it is written of me: I delight to do your will, O my God.’
translation.original_text: ὑπομένων ὑπέμεινα τὸν Κύριον καὶ προσέσχεν μοι

translator_notes.note: The Septuagint renders the Hebrew “you have dug ears for me” as “a body you have prepared for me” (σῶμα δὲ κατηρτίσω μοι). This Septuagint reading, quoted in Hebrews 10:5, became foundational for the Christian doctrine of incarnation.
